original: You step through the wood-paneled door and into a round room all done in even more fake wood paneling, making it look like you’re surrounded by dozens of tree trunks. The upper parts of the walls are painted to look like treetops, complete with patches of blue sky peeking through, and the entire place feels like you’ve stepped out of Tavros Station and right into a forest. The illusion is broken by a tall silver machine standing in the center of the room, with a large monitor on one side and some kind of scanning device sticking out of the top. A padded reclining chair stands to one side of the machine, made for someone to sit down and get comfortable, while a flat table that’s large enough to hold most people stands on the other side. A door marked ‘Staff Only’ stands closed in the middle of a tree trunk to the left. The wall to the right lacks the wood paneling, instead holding a bunch of monitors showing all different kinds of tattoos. A counter stands in front of the wall of monitors, and a woman stands behind it, next to a simple tablet workstation. The woman looks human at first glance, but the round, brown-furred ears sticking up out of her mass of curly blue hair show she has some mixed heritage, as does the darkness around her eyes that fades outward to match her tan skin. She’s dressed very casually, with an open-sided shirt and black sports bra over her sizable chest, and both her shoulders are tattooed. The woman gives you a bright smile, and steps out from behind the counter. “Hey, what’s up? I’m Catalina, and this is Treetop Tattoos.” She seems very informal, but you suppose getting a tattoo isn’t exactly like making a business deal. “What’s your name?” You tell her your name, and if she recognizes your last name, she doesn’t show it. And now that she’s not behind the counter, you can see that Catalina’s wearing tight athletic shorts over some strong legs; it looks like she spends plenty of time working out or used to be some kind of athlete. More tattoos, black leopard spots, rise up over both her hips. And that’s not all you can see – the woman’s packing a bulge at the front, with two sizable balls. She seems very casual about just having it out there. “Good to meet you,” Catalina says, still smiling. “So, like you probably guessed, we do tattoos here. I can ink mostly anything on mostly anyone, and it’s all done with some pretty basic microsurgeons, so there’s no worry about infection, and it’s easy to fix up or remove if you change your mind.” She shoots you a grin. “I got to get that out there right away, so many people come in here thinking it’s a lifetime thing when it’s like, no, you can get inked up for a weekend or a night out and then get it all taken out before you’ve got to go back to work.” Good to know. You tell her you’ve never had a nanotech tattoo before, and her brown eyes light up. “You picked a great place to start.” Catalina steps back behind her counter, and taps on her tablet. The monitors behind her swap to line drawings on a white background, displaying all kinds of art, ranging from spaceships to abstracts to things you’d see on a poster in a red light district. “This is most of what I do,” she says. “I get lots of people here who want something badass or something sexy, or a special picture for a special thing.” She picks up a stylus from the counter, and twirls it between her fingers, the movements quick and deft. “But I can draw just about anything you want, and we’ll scan you and get a pic to see what it looks like before the ink drops. What d’you think?”

original: You ask Catalina to tell you about the shop, as she’s clearly put a lot into it. “It’s pretty fucking cool, isn’t it?” she says, then spreads her arms, gesturing to the entire place. When you don’t say anything, she lets her arms drop, and shrugs. “Okay, it’s gonna be great someday, I’m just getting started. I figure once people start learning about the place, it’ll become a Tavros mainstay, and then I’ll be set.” She’s definitely in the right place for it. With all the travelers and Rushers coming through, there are bound to be plenty of people who want to get inked. “That’s the hope, [pc.name],” Catalina says. “I wanted to make it stand out, y’know, really catch some eyes as people walk through this place. That’s why I went with the treetop thing, because nobody thinks they’re gonna find a forest when they’re on a space station.” She leans one hip against the counter again, and looks up at the painted trees, a calmer expression on her face. “That’s not the only reason why, though. I mean, it works for this place, but I think any shop I opened up would have looked like this. The forest’s special to me, that’s why I got this ink.” She turns and shows you a tree of life on her right shoulder, done all in dark green. “Ever been to Jitafore?” You can’t say that you have, unless it was so long ago that you don’t remember. “It’s amazing,” Catalina says, and starts to grin. “It’s my kui-tan mom’s home world, so we headed there like once a year when I was growing up, to see the family and stuff like that. So much forest, just going on and on forever. I remember the first time I went there, I was maybe six, and it was like I could just get lost in the forests, because everything was so damn big.” She pauses, and narrows her eyes a little. “Though a bunch of the kids there called me stub-tail because my tail’s so short, but they can fuck off. Damn kids.” So that’s why she went with the forest theme for the shop? It must have taken some time to find someone to paint the place like this. “Not so long as you’d think,” she says, giving you a considering look. “I started making connections, like, right away once I decided to start up here. Finding artists took a little while, but I had credits and big damn walls, so it wasn’t too hard to find someone who could do some good forests.” She shrugs. “But yeah, I wanted to make a place that reminded me of that moon. It’s been way too long since I went back.” That’s too bad. And she’s kind of stuck here running the shop now, isn’t she? “Not forever,” Catalina says, a determined look on her face. She stands up a little straighter, instead of leaning against the counter. “I’ve got plans for this place. Step one, get myself established. Shouldn’t be too hard, like I said, since there’s a ton of people running through this station and I’m the only one doing ink.” She holds up her hand, curls out one finger, then a second. “Step two, get big enough to turn the place into a real house of ink, so it’s not just me working here.” She gestures to the wall opposite her. “I checked into it, and there’s room to expand. So if I make enough credits and find some other reliable inkers, I can make this forest grow.” And step three? Catalina smirks as she holds up a third finger. “Step three is always the same: profit!” She cackles, then gets serious again all at once. “But seriously, making a bunch of credits with this place is the major goal. I’m not thinking franchises, but give me a few years and I bet I can have tattoo fans from all over space coming here to get inked, not just people passing through. Once I’ve got a set and steady customer base and I’m reeling in the credits, I can move to step four.” So she’s made plans beyond profit? That’s more than some business plans you’ve seen. “Of course,” Catalina says, then huffs out a breath. “Whole point of working a lot right now is so I can make tons of credits and not have to work when I’m older. Once I’ve got this place rolling in, I’ll sell it. Maybe to whoever’s working here and wants to keep it going, maybe to some company who wants to buy it, who knows. Whatever gets me enough credits to head out and see a bunch of new worlds and make art for myself while I’m there. That’s the dream,” she adds, holding out a fourth finger. That’s quite the goal. And she’ll need a lot of credits to travel the galaxy. You carefully don’t mention that you personally know how much it all costs thanks to how you were brought up, but there’s no point in not telling her the obvious. “I know, I know,” she says, and rolls her eyes. “Both my moms are in finance, they got me the loan for this place. Mostly because we’ve heard horror stories about the people who do loans here on the station.” She shivers a little, though it looks like she’s being dramatic. “But yeah, I grew up with stock reports for bedtime stories. Maybe I’ll never be rich, but I can make this work for the life I want.” It sounds like a good goal, so long as she’s realistic about it. And if this place takes off, she could get there sooner than she thinks. “That’s the goal.” Catalina looks around the small studio, a wistful expression on her tanned face. “I mean, I’m a lot more about the art than the business stuff. My moms made me learn finance because they knew it’d make life easier for me. But having enough creds to not even need the business anymore, and just go travel and draw? That’s the life, [pc.name]. That’s the fucking best life.” It’s a good dream. And with the Planet Rush going on, there’ll be new places to go once she’s able to travel. “Ooh, I know,” she says, excitement shining in her eyes. “I’ve had some Rushers come through, and they told me about the places they’ve been to. Some of those new worlds sound so cool! I can’t wait to get out there, even if it’s gonna be years until then.”

original: You ask Catalina how she got into tattooing, and how long she’s been a tattoo artist. Her brown eyes light up, and she leans forward over the counter, with the air of someone who’s just been asked about their favorite subject. “Okay, so,” she begins, then giggles. “This might take a while.” You’ve got time. “So, I’ve been drawing since I was a little kid,” Catalina says. “That and sports were the only things that got me through school. And this one teacher I had who was, like, more excited about math than anyone should be, but that’s different.” She waves her hand like she’s brushing the subject change away. “I wanted to be an artist when I was a kid, but it was also like, how am I gonna make that work? Lots of people make art, but there’s not always steady money in that, and my moms were set on me making sure I had a job that paid.” You can understand that, which is about all you can say without getting into your own past. But you still had to take up a trade and work for your credits. “I figured I was shit out of luck after a while, because while I’ve always been good, I was never spectacular, y’know? Never fancy art school material or the kind of person that makes everyone go wow, I love your stuff, please take all of my credits. But when one of my friends saw this monster I was drawing and was like, that would make a kick-ass tattoo, it hit me, that was what I could do. So I started figuring out what I needed to do that.” She gestures at the leopard-spot tattoos on her hips, visible over the top of her athletic shorts. “I got these first, from the guy who taught me how to do tattoos,” she says. “I wanted something simple but still kinda wild. But I worked with that guy all through college, he had a shop near where I went to school, and by the time I was done, I was about ready to do this thing on my own.” So what were those early days like? And what was the first tattoo she ever gave? Catalina grimaces, and looks away, like she’s embarrassed. “I was so fucking nervous the first time I inked someone,” she says, then shakes her head. “This was an old-school shop, not the nanotech thing like I do here.” She gestures at the machine at the center of the shop. “I dunno why he worked that way, maybe he liked it being actually permanent, or mostly permanent. Whatever. My first guy was this big ausar dude, inked like crazy all over, and I’d seen him in the shop a few times, so he knew I was still learning. But when he asked the guy who owned the place if I was ready, he said I was, and... and I had to do, like, every relaxation technique I knew to keep my hands from shaking when I inked him.” She lets out a breath slowly. “But it turned out good, he was real happy with it.” What was the picture? “Kaithrit girl with a big ol’ ass,” Catalina says, then laughs. “He had one with this giant set of tits tattooed over his left shoulder blade, so he wanted one to balance it out.” She shakes her head again, giggling. “I’ve tattooed plenty of butts since then, but I think that’s still the biggest butt I’ve ever drawn.” So that was with old tattoo tools, and actual ink. What about the machine here, how does that work? “Oh, this thing is so cool,” she says, her eyes lighting up again. She taps the tablet sitting on her counter. “I draw something here, and then the machine scans you wherever you want it, so we can see how it’s gonna look before we get started. That makes so many people happy, you wouldn’t believe how excited they get once they know how it’ll turn out. But once you’re good with the drawing and where it’s going, I put the machine’s nano-injector thingy in place, and it goes to work.” Catalina grins, looking at the machine in the center of the shop with pride in her eyes. “And the best thing is, it’s a ton faster than old-school inking. I get people in here who want big pieces, and the longest it takes is maybe an hour. I had someone in here when I’d barely gotten started who wanted an entire back piece. It took me longer to draw it than it did to get them inked.” Has she tested it on herself? “Oh, of course,” Catalina says, giving you a stern look that seems wildly out of place on her expressive face. “That’s like a law in this business, you don’t use anything on clients you didn’t test on yourself. That’s like trusting a vegetarian to make your steak.” She turns and shows you her left shoulder. There’s some kind of art done there, though it looks like a collection of lines with a ridiculous number of angles and you can’t make any sense of what it’s supposed to be. “You ever heard of Titanslayer? They’re

original: You do have enough credits, so everything’s good there. Does she get a lot of people who come in trying to get something for free? “All the damn time,” Catalina says, rolling her eyes. “I’ve heard so many drunk sob stories from people who ‘have to get inked right now, because they just have to.’” She draws out the words, making them sound agonized, then laughs. “But since you’re not doing that, let’s talk about getting you some ink.” With another tap from Catalina’s stylus, the screens behind her counter switch to displays of the machine that’s standing in the middle of the tattoo shop, surrounded by example tattoos on different skin types, including scales and fur. “So, let me go through how this whole thing works before we get started, so you know what you’re getting into. It’s mostly simple and like a hundred percent safe, but there’s a bunch of stuff to go over.” Catalina clears her throat, assuming a much more businesslike stance instead of her usual casual pose. “So, the main thing is that the tattoo comes from tiny nanobots that the machine’s gonna inject under your skin,” she begins. “They’re mostly inert, and all they do is color the skin right above them and whatever’s coming out of it. That’s why they work on fur and scales, and not just skin like mine.” She pauses. “The bad part is that this doesn’t work on feathers, because the base of the feathers is too far from the rest of them. So all you get is this patch of colored feathers, and you can do that with dye.” Another tap of her stylus, and the screens change to show the machine in use. The scanner at the top comes down on a long, flexible cord, and positions itself above an arm with pale skin. “I’ll draw what you want, and once you’re good with the design, I send it into the machine. It’ll scan wherever you’re getting inked, so I hope you’re not shy about getting a little exposed.” She grins. “Once the scan’s done, I can show you what the tattoo will look like when it’s on. We can still make changes, but once you’re good with what’s on the screen, I get started and you get inked.” The screens’ display changes one more time, showing the scanner positioned over the pale arm, with dozens of tendrils coming down around it. The tendrils place themselves all along the person’s arm, and start to quiver, their tips barely touching the skin. When they move away, the first lines of a tattoo are left behind, long dark lines that slowly form a picture. “And that’s pretty much it,” Catalina says, looking at you. “Most people don’t have a reaction to the nanotech, but I’ve got an emergency call button for the station’s medical service, so if something goes horribly wrong I can get someone here real quick.” Her usual smile looks a little strained around the edges. “There is so much legal bullshit around running this place, I swear, but I’d also be so fucked if someone had a seizure on my table, so it’s worth it.” Are there any other issues you should worry about? You have some nanotech of your own in your system, and it would cause problems if the tattoo microsurgeons interfered with that. “Oh, no worries there,” Catalina says. “Like I said, these things are mostly inert, they just do a pigment change. There’s nothing in them that should, like, trigger any kind of immune reaction, and nobody’s found a species that’s got problems with them yet.” She looks a little tense for a moment. “But I am gonna limit you to one tattoo at a time, just to make sure. You good with that?” That’s fine. If nothing goes wrong, you can always get more. Anything else? “Couple policy bits,” she says, then holds up one finger. “First, if you’ve got any symbiotic or parasitic organisms on you, I won’t tattoo those, because those things are basically animals and can’t give informed consent.” She gives you a stern look. “Yes, I’m serious, and yes, people argue with me about this. Second,” Catalina continues, raising another finger, “You need your tattoo removed, I’ll do it for free, no questions asked.” That seems unusual. Why is that? She drops her hand and heaves a sigh. “I read about some obscure as fuck lawsuit when I was getting this place set up. Turns out, someone got a tattoo a couple days before a job interview and couldn’t afford to get it removed, and they sued the artist because the tattoo kept them from getting the job. I don’t know how the hell it worked that way, but I’m not taking a chance on losing this place thanks to some sue-happy jackass. So I’ll take out the microsurgeons for free. But it takes like ten minutes, so it’s not like it cuts into my day when it happens.” She shrugs. “Any other questions?” It sounds like she’s covered everything. “Sweet.” Catalina leans forward over the counter, making her boobs bounce a little in the heavy sports bra holding them in place. “So, you ready to get inked?”
